questions.question: What does DDPM offer that GANs and VAEs each lack?
options: Faster sampling than both
A single stable loss (no minimax saddle point like GANs) that bounds log p(x) (likelihoods, unlike GANs) while producing SOTA-quality samples (unlike blurry VAEs)
Smaller models than both
Training without any data
questions.explanation: Sohl-Dickstein (2015) had the theory; Ho, Jain, Abbeel (2020) simplified the loss to one line — predict the noise — and by 2022 it became Stable Diffusion. In 2026 it is the substrate.

questions.question: What closed form makes the forward noising process tractable?
options: Noise must be added one step at a time
The cumulative noising step is itself Gaussian: q(x_t | x_0) = N(√ᾱ_t · x_0, (1−ᾱ_t)·I) — so you can jump from clean data to ANY noise level t in one shot during training
The noise is uniform, not Gaussian
x_t equals x_0 scaled by t
questions.explanation: With β_t from 1e-4 to 0.02 over T=1000 steps, x_T is approximately N(0, I). This one-shot property is why each training step is cheap: sample x_0, pick random t, add noise, regress.

questions.question: What is the DDPM training loss in practice?
options: A weighted sum of 1000 KL terms
L_simple = E[||ε − ε_θ(√ᾱ_t·x_0 + √(1−ᾱ_t)·ε, t)||²] — sample data, timestep, and noise; predict the noise; MSE. No minimax, no reparameterization machinery
Cross-entropy on pixel classes
An adversarial discriminator score
questions.explanation: The full ELBO's per-timestep KL terms reduce to noise-prediction MSE with specific coefficients; Ho dropped the coefficients ("simple" loss) and quality actually improved.

questions.question: Why does training one denoiser across all noise levels work so well?
options: Denoising is harder than generating

At t=T the task is trivial, at t=0 only minor cleanup remains, and intermediate steps share gradients through the same weights from every noise level — denoising is easy while one-shot generating is hard
Each timestep gets its own network
Noise levels are independent problems
questions.explanation: Generation is decomposed into a thousand easy denoising problems. The reverse chain just iterates from pure noise to a clean sample.

questions.question: What is the score-matching connection?
options: Diffusion models compute FID during training

Vincent (2011) proved noise prediction is equivalent to estimating the score ∇_x log q(x_t|x_0) — the reverse process walks up the density gradient, a guided random walk toward high-probability regions
Scores refer to discriminator outputs
There is no connection to scores
questions.explanation: This equivalence is what links DDPM to the score-based SDE view (Song & Ermon) and ultimately to flow matching — same gradient field, different path.
